data.content:
### YAML Configuration for README Root Style

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/README-GENERATION.md

Configures the primary README style written to the repository root (README.md). Available options include 'extra', 'classic', 'awesome', and 'flat'. This setting dictates which README style is presented as the main file, while all generated styles are kept in the README_ALTERNATIVES/ directory.

```yaml
readme:
  root_style: extra  # Options: extra, classic, awesome, flat
```

--------------------------------

### Issue Submission Form Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/HOW_IT_WORKS.md

YAML configuration for the GitHub issue template used for resource submissions. It defines a structured form with required fields and specifies automatic labeling and input validation.

```yaml
# .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/submit-resource.yml
- Structured form with all required fields
- Auto-labels with "resource-submission"
- Validates input formats
```

--------------------------------

### NVM User Configuration for Perplexity MCP Server (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Perplexity-MCP/CLAUDE.md

Provides configuration for Claude Desktop when using Node Version Manager (NVM). It requires absolute paths for both the Node.js executable and the server script to ensure correct execution.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "perplexity": {
      "command": "/Users/username/.nvm/versions/node/v16.x.x/bin/node",
      "args": [
        "/Users/username/path/to/perplexity-mcp/server.js"
      ],
      "env": {
        "PERPLEXITY_API_KEY": "your_perplexity_api_key"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Perplexity MCP Server with npx (Claude Desktop JSON)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Perplexity-MCP/CLAUDE.md

Configures the Perplexity MCP server in Claude Desktop when installed globally. Uses 'npx' to run the installed package, simplifying path management.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "perplexity": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "perplexity-mcp"
      ],
      "env": {
        "PERPLEXITY_API_KEY": "your_perplexity_api_key"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configuration Files for README Generation (YAML)

Source: https://context7.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/llms.txt

YAML configuration files are used to control the README generation process and define styles. The `acc-config.yaml` file specifies the root README style and lists available styles with their properties. The `categories.yaml` file defines the structure and metadata for different categories and subcategories of resources.

```yaml
# acc-config.yaml - Global configuration
readme:
  root_style: awesome  # Which style becomes README.md (extra, classic, awesome, flat)

styles:
  awesome:
    name: Awesome
    badge: badge-style-awesome.svg
    highlight_color: "#cc3366"
    filename: README_AWESOME.md
  extra:
    name: Extra
    badge: badge-style-extra.svg
    highlight_color: "#6a6a8a"
    filename: README_EXTRA.md
  # ... more styles

style_order:  # Badge display order
  - awesome
  - extra
  - classic
  - flat

# templates/categories.yaml - Category definitions
categories:
  - id: tooling
    name: "Tooling"
    prefix: tool
    icon: "🧰"
    description: "Tooling denotes applications..."
    order: 3
    subcategories:
      - id: general
        name: "General"
      - id: ide-integrations
        name: "IDE Integrations"

```

--------------------------------

### Configure Perplexity MCP Server in Claude Desktop (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Perplexity-MCP/CLAUDE.md

Adds the Perplexity MCP server configuration to the Claude Desktop settings. This allows Claude Desktop to communicate with the local Perplexity MCP server. Requires the absolute path to the server script.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "perplexity": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": [
        "/absolute/path/to/perplexity-mcp/server.js"
      ],
      "env": {
        "PERPLEXITY_API_KEY": "your_perplexity_api_key"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Python Logging Configuration with Loguru

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/claude-code-mcp-enhanced/CLAUDE.md

Configures the loguru logger to write messages to a file named 'app.log' and includes log rotation based on file size (10 MB). This setup ensures efficient log management for applications.

```python
from loguru import logger

# Configure logger
logger.add("app.log", rotation="10 MB")
```

--------------------------------

### YAML Configuration for README Style Selector

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/README-GENERATION.md

Defines metadata for each README style used in the style selector. This includes the display name for alt text, the badge filename, the highlight color when selected, and the corresponding filename within README_ALTERNATIVES/. The 'style_order' list controls the visual order of badges.

```yaml
styles:
  extra:
    name: Extra                    # Display name for badge alt text
    badge: badge-style-extra.svg   # Badge filename in assets/
    highlight_color: "#6a6a8a"     # Border color when selected
    filename: README_EXTRA.md

  classic:
    name: Classic
    badge: badge-style-classic.svg
    highlight_color: "#c9a227"
    filename: README_CLASSIC.md
  # ... other styles

style_order:
  - extra
  - classic
  - flat
  - awesome
```

--------------------------------

### Vibe Tools Ask Command Options

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Cursor-Tools/CLAUDE.md

Configuration options for the `vibe-tools ask` command. Users can specify the AI provider, the model to use, and the reasoning effort for supported models to control the depth of AI responses.

```bash
--provider=<provider>: AI provider to use (openai, anthropic, perplexity, gemini, modelbox, openrouter, or xai)
--model=<model>: Model to use (required for the ask command)
--reasoning-effort=<low|medium|high>: Control the depth of reasoning for supported models (OpenAI o1/o3-mini models and Claude 3.7 Sonnet). Higher values produce more thorough responses for complex questions.
```

--------------------------------

### Setting Custom Async Client Factory

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Basic-Memory/CLAUDE.md

This Python code shows how to configure a custom factory for the asynchronous client, typically for cloud application integration. By setting a custom factory before importing MCP tools, dependency injection can be enabled for cloud consolidation and advanced configurations.

```python
from basic_memory.mcp import async_client

# Set custom factory before importing tools
async_client.set_client_factory(your_custom_factory)

```

--------------------------------

### Set Perplexity API Key (Environment Variable)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Perplexity-MCP/CLAUDE.md

Configures the Perplexity API key, which is required for the server to authenticate with the Perplexity API. This can be done via a .env file or directly as an environment variable.

```bash
PERPLEXITY_API_KEY=your_api_key_here
```

--------------------------------

### Setup Computer-Use Demo Environment

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/official-documentation/Anthropic-Quickstarts/CLAUDE.md

Commands to set up the environment, build the Docker image, and run the Computer-Use Demo container. It requires an ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and mounts local directories for code and configuration.

```bash
./setup.sh
docker build . -t computer-use-demo:local
docker run -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -v $(pwd)/computer_use_demo:/home/computeruse/computer_use_demo/ -v $HOME/.anthropic:/home/computeruse/.anthropic -p 5900:5900 -p 8501:8501 -p 6080:6080 -p 8080:8080 -it computer-use-demo:local
```

--------------------------------

### NPM Global Installation of Perplexity MCP Server

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Perplexity-MCP/CLAUDE.md

Installs the Perplexity MCP Server globally, making it available as a command-line tool. After installation, it can be configured in Claude Desktop using 'npx'.

```bash
npm install -g .
```

--------------------------------

### Python Script for README Generation

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/README-GENERATION.md

The core script responsible for generating various README styles and assets based on data sources like CSV files and configuration YAMLs. It handles updating resource entries, creating badges, and writing output files.

```python
python3 scripts/readme/generate_readme.py
```

--------------------------------

### Vibe Tools Plan Command Options

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Cursor-Tools/CLAUDE.md

Options to customize the AI providers and models used for file identification and plan generation in the `vibe-tools plan` command. It also supports incorporating external documentation via a URL.

```bash
--fileProvider=<provider>: Provider for file identification (gemini, openai, anthropic, perplexity, modelbox, openrouter, or xai)
--thinkingProvider=<provider>: Provider for plan generation (gemini, openai, anthropic, perplexity, modelbox, openrouter, or xai)
--fileModel=<model>: Model to use for file identification
--thinkingModel=<model>: Model to use for plan generation
--with-doc=<doc_url>: Fetch content from a document URL and include it as context for both file identification and planning (e.g., `vibe-tools plan "implement feature X following the spec" --with-doc=https://example.com/feature-spec`)
```

--------------------------------

### Update README Tree (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/tools/readme_tree/README.md

Executes the Python script to update the file tree within the README. It targets the section between specific start and end markers defined in the configuration.

```bash
python tools/readme_tree/update_readme_tree.py
```

--------------------------------

### Implementation Planning with Vibe Tools

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Cursor-Tools/CLAUDE.md

Generates a focused implementation plan for a given query. This command leverages multiple AI models to identify relevant files, extract their content, and then generate a detailed plan. Default providers are Gemini for file identification and OpenAI o3-mini for plan generation.

```bash
vibe-tools plan "<query>"
```

--------------------------------

### Deployment Commands

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/SG-Cars-Trends-Backend/CLAUDE.md

Commands for deploying the infrastructure, API, and web application to different environments.

```APIDOC
## Deployment Commands

Commands for deploying the project's infrastructure, API, and web application.

### Infrastructure Deployment
- `pnpm deploy:dev`: Deploy all infrastructure components to the development environment.
- `pnpm deploy:staging`: Deploy all infrastructure components to the staging environment.
- `pnpm deploy:prod`: Deploy all infrastructure components to the production environment.

### API Deployment
- `pnpm deploy:api:dev`: Deploy the API to the development environment.
- `pnpm deploy:api:staging`: Deploy the API to the staging environment.
- `pnpm deploy:api:prod`: Deploy the API to the production environment.

### Web Deployment
- `pnpm deploy:web:dev`: Deploy the web application to the development environment.
- `pnpm deploy:web:staging`: Deploy the web application to the staging environment.
- `pnpm deploy:web:prod`: Deploy the web application to the production environment.
```

--------------------------------

### JavaScript Typewriter Text Generator

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/development/vintage-manual-animation-style-guide.md

A JavaScript function to dynamically generate SVG text elements for a typewriter effect. It calculates character positions and animates opacity based on configurable delays and cycle durations.

```javascript
function generateTypewriterText(text, x, y, options = {}) {
  const {
    charWidth = 24,
    charDelay = 0.3,
    cycleDuration = 6,
    fontSize = 36
  } = options;

  const chars = text.split('');
  const totalChars = chars.length;

  return chars.map((char, i) => {
    const charX = x + (i * charWidth);
    const appearTime = (i * charDelay) / cycleDuration;
    const values = Array(20).fill('1');
    const appearIndex = Math.floor(appearTime * 20);
    for (let j = 0; j < appearIndex; j++) values[j] = '0';

    return `
      <text x="${charX}" y="${y}" opacity="0">${char}
        <animate attributeName="opacity"
                 values="${values.join(';')}"
                 dur="${cycleDuration}s"
                 repeatCount="indefinite"/>
      </text>`;
  }).join('
');
}
```

--------------------------------

### Template Placeholders and Resolution

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/README-GENERATION.md

Demonstrates the use of `{{PLACEHOLDER}}` syntax for dynamic content in templates. It explains how asset paths are tokenized and resolved based on the output location, walking up to the repository root to find the `pyproject.toml` file.

```markdown
Templates use `{{PLACEHOLDER}}` syntax for dynamic content. Key placeholders:

| Placeholder | Description | Generator Method |
|-------------|-------------|
| `{{ASSET_PATH('file.svg')}}` | Tokenized asset path resolved per output location | `resolve_asset_tokens()` |
| `{{STYLE_SELECTOR}}` | "Pick Your Style" badge row linking to all README variants | `get_style_selector()` |
| `{{REPO_TICKER}}` | Animated SVG ticker showing featured projects | `generate_repo_ticker()` |
| `{{ANNOUNCEMENTS}}` | Latest announcements from `templates/announcements.yaml` | `load_announcements()` |
| `{{WEEKLY_SECTION}}` | Latest additions section | `generate_weekly_section()` |
| `{{TABLE_OF_CONTENTS}}` | Table of contents | `generate_toc()` |
| `{{BODY_SECTIONS}}` | Main resource listings | `generate_section_content()` |
| `{{FOOTER}}` | Footer template content | `load_footer()` |

Template content outside these placeholders is treated as manual copy and is not regenerated.

Asset references use token placeholders (e.g. `{{ASSET_PATH('logo.svg')}}`) that are resolved after templating based on the destination README path. Resolution walks upward to the repo root (`pyproject.toml`) and computes a relative path to `/assets` for each output file.

Generated outputs are prefixed with `<!-- GENERATED FILE: do not edit directly -->` as a reminder that edits belong in templates and source data.
```

--------------------------------

### Validate Individual Resource URLs and Fetch Metadata (Python)

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/scripts/README.md

This script validates individual resources, typically URLs, and fetches associated metadata. It's utilized in workflows for validating user submissions and manual validation processes. The function `validate_single_resource` can handle both regular URLs and GitHub repository links, using keyword arguments for configuration.

```python
import requests
from github import Github
from urllib.parse import urlparse

# Assuming helper functions like parse_github_url and get_github_client are available
# from utils.github_utils import parse_github_url, get_github_client

def validate_single_resource(**kwargs):
    url = kwargs.get('url')
    if not url:
        return {"status": "error", "message": "URL is required"}

    resource_data = {"url": url}
    is_github = "github.com" in urlparse(url).netloc

    try:
        if is_github:
            # Logic to parse GitHub URL and use GitHub API
            repo_api_url = parse_github_url(url)
            if repo_api_url:
                g = get_github_client() # Ensure get_github_client is imported/defined
                try:
                    repo = g.get_repo(repo_api_url.split('/')[-1]) # Simplified: assumes repo name is last part
                    resource_data.update({
                        "name": repo.full_name,
                        "stars": repo.stargazers_count,
                        "description": repo.description,
                        "license": repo.license.name if repo.license else None,
                        "last_modified": repo.pushed_at.isoformat() if repo.pushed_at else None
                    })
                    return {"status": "success", "data": resource_data}
                except Exception as e: # Catch specific PyGithub exceptions
                    return {"status": "error", "message": f"Failed to fetch GitHub repo data: {e}"}
            else:
                return {"status": "error", "message": "Invalid GitHub URL format"}
        else:
            # Logic for validating regular URLs
            response = requests.head(url, allow_redirects=True, timeout=10)
            response.raise_for_status()
            resource_data.update({
                "status_code": response.status_code,
                "content_type": response.headers.get('content-type')
            })
            return {"status": "success", "data": resource_data}

    except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
        return {"status": "error", "message": f"URL validation failed: {e}"}
    except Exception as e:
        return {"status": "error", "message": f"An unexpected error occurred: {e}"}

# Example usage:
# print(validate_single_resource(url="https://github.com/user/repo"))
# print(validate_single_resource(url="https://example.com"))
```

It supports configurable root styles, dynamic placeholders for selection and repo tickers, hierarchical table of contents, and preserves custom sections. It also integrates GitHub Stats for collapsible repository statistics.

```python
from pygithub_util import parse_github_url

```

--------------------------------

### Add Dependencies to package.json

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Course-Builder/CLAUDE.md

Demonstrates how to add new dependencies to a package.json file while maintaining alphabetical order. This method uses string replacement to insert the new dependency without overwriting existing entries, ensuring the list remains sorted.

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "@coursebuilder/utils-media": "1.0.0",
    "@coursebuilder/utils-seo": "1.0.0"
  }
}

// Replace with:
{
  "dependencies": {
    "@coursebuilder/utils-media": "1.0.0",
    "@coursebuilder/utils-resource": "1.0.0", // New line added here
    "@coursebuilder/utils-seo": "1.0.0"
  }
}
```

It integrates with the GitHub API to check repositories, detect licenses, and fetch last modified dates. It includes exponential backoff for rate limiting and supports overrides from a YAML configuration file, outputting results in JSON format for CI/CD.

```python
import csv
import requests
import time
from tqdm import tqdm
from github import Github

# ... (rest of the script logic for link validation)
```

### Database Commands

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/SG-Cars-Trends-Backend/CLAUDE.md

Commands for managing database migrations and schema.

```APIDOC
## Database Commands

Commands to manage database migrations and schema.

### Commands
- `pnpm db:migrate`: Run pending database migrations.
- `pnpm db:migrate:check`: Check for pending database migrations.
- `pnpm db:generate`: Generate database migration files.
- `pnpm db:push`: Push the database schema to the database.
- `pnpm db:drop`: Drop the entire database.
```

--------------------------------

### Repository Command Options

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Cursor-Tools/CLAUDE.md

Options specific to analyzing GitHub repositories, including specifying the source repository, subdirectory, and external documents for context.

```APIDOC
## Repository Command Options

### Description
Options for analyzing GitHub repositories. This command can be invoked using the nickname 'Gemini'.

### Method
`vibe-tools repo ...`

### Endpoint
N/A

### Parameters
#### Repository Specific Options
- `--provider=<provider>` (string) - Optional - AI provider to use (gemini, openai, openrouter, perplexity, modelbox, anthropic, or xai).
- `--model=<model name>` (string) - Optional - Model to use for repository analysis.
- `--max-tokens=<number>` (integer) - Optional - Maximum tokens for response.
- `--from-github=<GitHub username>/<repository name>[@<branch>]` (string) - Required - Analyze a remote GitHub repository without cloning it locally.
- `--subdir=<path>` (string) - Optional - Analyze a specific subdirectory instead of the entire repository.
- `--with-doc=<doc_url>` (string) - Optional - Fetch content from a document URL and include it as context.
```

--------------------------------

### README Generator System: Generating README Files

Source: https://context7.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/llms.txt

This system utilizes the Template Method pattern for generating README files in various styles. A base `ReadmeGenerator` class handles common logic, while style-specific subclasses implement custom formatting. It supports generating specific styles like 'Awesome' or 'Flat', with options for filtering and sorting, and can also generate the root README based on configuration.

```python
from scripts.readme.generators.visual import VisualReadmeGenerator
from scripts.readme.generators.awesome import AwesomeReadmeGenerator
from scripts.readme.generators.flat import ParameterizedFlatListGenerator

# Generate a specific README style
generator = AwesomeReadmeGenerator(
    csv_path="THE_RESOURCES_TABLE.csv",
    template_dir="templates",
    assets_dir="assets",
    repo_root="/path/to/repo"
)

# Generate and get resource count + backup path
resource_count, backup_path = generator.generate()
# Output: README_ALTERNATIVES/README_AWESOME.md with 164+ resources

# Generate flat list view with category filter and sort
flat_generator = ParameterizedFlatListGenerator(
    csv_path="THE_RESOURCES_TABLE.csv",
    template_dir="templates",
    assets_dir="assets",
    repo_root="/path/to/repo",
    category_slug="tooling",  # Filter: 'all', 'tooling', 'hooks', etc.
    sort_type="updated"       # Sort: 'az', 'updated', 'created', 'releases'
)
flat_generator.generate()
# Output: README_ALTERNATIVES/README_FLAT_TOOLING_UPDATED.md

# Generate root README (uses configured root_style from acc-config.yaml)
from scripts.readme.generate_readme import build_root_generator
root_gen = build_root_generator("awesome", csv_path, template_dir, assets_dir, repo_root)
root_gen.generate(output_path="README.md")
```

--------------------------------

### Validate Resource Links (Python CLI)

Source: https://context7.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/llms.txt

The link validation system verifies resource URLs, retrieves GitHub metadata, and updates a CSV file with the current status. It supports various command-line options for full validation, limiting the number of links, running in GitHub Actions mode, and validating single resources. Overrides can be configured in a YAML file to prevent automatic updates of specific fields.

```bash
# Command line usage for full validation
# python -m scripts.validation.validate_links

# With max links limit
# python -m scripts.validation.validate_links --max-links 50

# GitHub Action mode (JSON output)
# python -m scripts.validation.validate_links --github-action

# Single resource validation
# python -m scripts.validation.validate_single_resource "https://github.com/user/repo"
```

```yaml

# Override configuration (templates/resource-overrides.yaml)
# Allows locking fields to prevent automatic updates:
"""
overrides:
  tool-abc12345:
    license: "MIT"           # Locked - won't be updated by validation
    active: "TRUE"           # Locked - resource stays active
    skip_validation: false   # Set true to skip entirely
    notes: "Manually verified license"
"""

```

### Install Dev Dependencies with Make

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/TESTING.md

Installs all necessary development dependencies for the project using the 'install' target in the Makefile. This is a prerequisite for running tests and other development tasks.

```bash

```

### Installing Project Dependencies

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/resources/claude.md-files/Basic-Memory/CLAUDE.md

This command installs the necessary project dependencies, including development tools. It can be executed using either 'make install' or by directly using pip with the editable install option.

```shell
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

--------------------------------

### Python Path Resolution Logic in ReadmeGenerator

Source: https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code/blob/main/docs/README-GENERATION.md

This Python code snippet illustrates key properties within the `ReadmeGenerator` base class that facilitate a dynamic path resolution system. This system correctly handles relative paths across different README file locations, ensuring assets and links are resolved properly.

```python
# In ReadmeGenerator base class:

is_root_style    # True if this style matches config's root_style
resolved_output_path  # Default output path (under README_ALTERNATIVES/)
alternative_output_path  # Path under README_ALTERNATIVES/ for this style
# Root generation uses generate(output_path="README.md")
```
